title: Contractor Tender Pack
description: Assemble a tender pack and evaluation approach for a building-services contract, including scope, service levels, safety requirements, reporting expectations, and vendor scorecard.
whyItMatters: Contractors deliver a huge share of the tenant experience and operational reliability of a property. A weak scope or poor evaluation method usually leads to price-driven decisions that cost more in failures, rework, and stakeholder frustration.
skillsLearned: Defining service levels and governance expectations
Balancing price, safety, and service quality

steps.title: Define the operating need
steps.description: Choose a contract area such as HVAC, cleaning, security, or vertical transport and document current pain points, asset context, and expected outcomes from the new contract.
steps.tips: A scope should solve a real operating problem, not just renew the incumbent by default.

steps.title: Write the tender scope
steps.description: Draft a scope covering service frequency, attendance requirements, reporting cadence, safety and compliance standards, escalation protocols, and tenant-facing behavior expectations.
steps.tips: Include what good looks like in practice, not just the tasks.

steps.title: Build the evaluation model
steps.description: Create a weighted scorecard covering price, technical capability, references, safety systems, communication quality, and reporting discipline.
steps.tips: A better tender pack makes trade-offs explicit before the quotes come in.

steps.title: Prepare the recommendation
steps.description: Summarize how bids would be compared, what evidence is required from vendors, and how the final recommendation would be presented to the owner or procurement lead.
steps.tips: Decision-makers need a transparent process as much as they need a contractor name.

rubric.description: The scope is specific enough that vendors know what success looks like and how it will be measured.
Safety, reporting, communication, and escalation expectations are clearly defined.
The scorecard produces a balanced assessment rather than a price-only comparison.
The tender approach could be defended confidently to an owner, procurement team, or audit review.
